text: The surgeon inserts a 5mm trocar into the left upper quadrant of the abdomen, preparing for laparoscopic access.
The surgeon visually inspects the abdomen and pelvis, noting the absence of gross contamination despite the presence of free air.
The surgeon inserts a 5mm trocar into the right lower quadrant and another into the right mid quadrant of the abdomen.
The surgeon begins to maneuver behind the uterus to access the first abscess, using laparoscopic instruments to navigate the pelvic region.
The uterus is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon holds the epiploic appendages on the sigmoid colon, stabilizing the tissue for further manipulation.
The surgeon attempts to access the lateral cavity, resulting in visible pus emerging from the area.
The surgeon pauses the procedure to take cultures from the area, likely near the abscess cavity.
The surgeon maneuvers behind the uterus, continuing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon begins to break into the lateral cavity, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon observes the inflamed rectum located behind the uterus, affected by the adjacent abscess cavity.
The surgeon exposes the abscess cavity, with the left ovary visibly displaced into the cavity.
The surgeon performs a lavage in the surgical area, followed by the placement of a drain to manage fluid accumulation.
The surgeon continues to work laterally, manipulating tissues in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a drain in the deep pelvis, curling it back with the tip positioned in the second cavity along the iliac vessels.
